figures.gloss: The weaver-saint of Banaras — born into a low-caste Muslim julaha family, singing nirguna devotion in blunt vernacular verse that mocked pandit and mullah alike: idol-worship and circumcision, Veda and Quran, all scolded from the loom.
figures.facet: Iconoclasm with a day job: Kabir's authority comes from low-caste labor and lived devotion rather than lineage or learning, and his ulatbamsi ('upside-down speech') poems weaponize paradox the way other traditions weaponize miracle.
figures.provenance: The Bijak; Kabir's bhagat bani in the Adi Granth; Charlotte Vaudeville, A Weaver Named Kabir (1993).

figures.gloss: Kabir's God carries the name Ram yet is explicitly not Dasharatha's son — unborn, formless, without attributes, beyond both Hindu and Muslim naming. Kabir uses Ram, Hari, Allah, and Khuda interchangeably for the one reality none of them contains.
figures.facet: The boldest move in bhakti: taking the avatar's own name and emptying it of the avatar — devotion's warmest word kept, its mythology deleted, so that Hindu and Muslim each hear their own God refused and offered back in the same line.
figures.provenance: Kabir's verse in the Bijak and Adi Granth ('the Ram I speak of was never born'); Vaudeville (1993) on Kabir's nirguna theology.

figures.gloss: 'The Seed' or 'the Inventory' — the eastern-recension scripture of the Kabir Panth, gathering Kabir's ramainis, shabdas, and sakhis into the book his followers treat as the map to the treasure of the self.
figures.facet: A scripture of anti-scripture: the panth canonized a poet who boasted he never touched ink or paper and jeered at the book-learned — the mirror's cleanest case of an oral iconoclast becoming the sacred text he warned against.
figures.provenance: The Bijak (eastern/Dharamdasi recension); Linda Hess and Shukdev Singh, The Bijak of Kabir (1983).

figures.gloss: The Word that pierces: in Kabir's poems the true guru looses the shabda like an arrow, and the unstruck sound (anahad nad) rings in the body of the devotee who turns inward. Devotion is routed through hearing, not through image or rite.
figures.facet: The sant stream's load-bearing concept — an interior, imageless channel to the divine open to any caste and either creed — which is exactly the piece the later Radhasoami masters lifted and systematized into a formal yoga.
figures.provenance: Shabda poems of the Bijak (Hess & Singh trans.); Vaudeville (1993) on sant devotional vocabulary.

figures.gloss: The town where Kabir chose to die. Tradition promised liberation to whoever died in Kashi and rebirth as a donkey to whoever died in cursed Magahar — so Kabir left Kashi at the end, deliberately, to die in the wrong place. Legend says his body became flowers, divided between Hindu and Muslim mourners; a samadhi and a tomb stand at Magahar side by side.
figures.facet: The last act as the last argument: salvation is not real estate. Kabir spent his death the way he spent his verse — breaking a ritual guarantee to prove the guarantee empty — and both communities' shrines over one absent body seal the point.
figures.provenance: Kabir hagiography and the Magahar verses (Vaudeville 1993); the paired samadhi and mazar at Magahar, Uttar Pradesh.

figures.gloss: A merchant convert of Kabir hagiography whose descendants at Damakheda (Chhattisgarh) hold the largest hereditary guru-lineage of the Kabir Panth. The Dharamdasi branch treats the Anurag Sagar as its central scripture and Dharamdas as effectively its founder.
figures.facet: The disciple who became the institution: unlike Kabir, who left no organized following by design, Dharamdas's line supplied exactly the hereditary structure — mahants, initiation rites, a fixed seat — that an itinerant weaver's iconoclasm never provided for itself.
figures.provenance: Kabir Panth hagiography of the Dharamdasi branch; David N. Lorenzen, Kabir Legends and Ananta-Das's Kabir Parachai (1991), on the Dharamdasi lineage at Damakheda.

figures.gloss: 'The Ocean of Love' — a dialogue in verse between Kabir and Dharamdas, composed within the panth and attributed to Kabir, narrating a developed cosmology: the soul's fall and captivity under Kal (the negative power ruling this creation) and its rescue through the true Sat Purush, whom Kabir claims to serve as an emissary sent age after age.
figures.facet: A scripture that outgrew its poet: the historical Kabir's blunt, image-breaking couplets give way here to an elaborate mythological system with named cosmic tiers and a rescuer-mission narrative — the panth's own theology doing constructive work its founder's verse never attempted.
figures.provenance: The Anurag Sagar (Dharamdasi transmission); its Sat Purush/Kal cosmology and its documented adoption into the Radhasoami stream discussed in Mark Juergensmeyer, Radhasoami Reality (1991).

figures.gloss: 'The True Person' — the real, formless God of the Anurag Sagar's cosmology, dwelling above and beyond Kal's counterfeit creation. Kabir is held by the panth to be Sat Purush's emissary, sent into the world age after age to call souls out of Kal's domain and back to their true origin.
figures.facet: A gnostic-shaped correction bolted onto bhakti: Sat Purush answers a question Kabir's own nirguna verse never quite poses — if the formless Ram is beyond all this-worldly attribute, who made and runs the visible world? — by supplying a lesser, deceptive creator-power (Kal) for the true God to be rescued from.
figures.provenance: The Anurag Sagar; Mark Juergensmeyer, Radhasoami Reality (1991), on Sat Purush/Kal cosmology and its descent into Sant Mat.

figures.gloss: The leatherworker-sant of Banaras (trad. 15th-16th c.), Kabir's near-contemporary in the low-caste nirguna devotional stream, whose hymns stand alongside Kabir's as bhagat bani in the Sikh Adi Granth (compiled 1604). His memory anchors the modern Ravidassia community, which has moved toward status as a distinct religion.
figures.facet: The other cobbler-saint: Ravidas's caste (chamar, leatherworker — ritually lower even than Kabir's weaver-julaha) made his devotional authority a sharper social claim, and it is precisely that caste-inflected legacy that has since carried his following furthest from the Kabir Panth's own institutional shadow.
figures.provenance: Ravidas's hymns (Adi Granth bhagat bani); Winand M. Callewaert & Peter Friedlander, The Life and Works of Raidas (1992).

figures.gloss: A cotton-carder of Gujarat and later Rajasthan (1544-1603), preacher of the formless Ram in Kabir's manner, whose Dadu Panth institutionalized itself much as the Kabir Panth did, with its own akharas and a scriptural anthology, the Dadu-vani. Dadu Panth compilers preserved a large body of sant-tradition verse, including material attributed to Kabir.
figures.facet: The archivist-saint: where Kabir's own panth mainly transmits Kabir, the Dadu Panth's greater service to the wider sant stream was custodial — its anthologies are a chief reason the nirguna poetry of Kabir, Ravidas, and Namdev survives in relatively early manuscript witnesses at all.
figures.provenance: The Dadu-vani and the Dadu Panth's Panch-vani/Sarvangi anthologies; Winand M. Callewaert, The Sarvangi of the Dadupanthi Rajab (1978); Monika Horstmann's studies of the Dadu Panth.

figures.gloss: A tailor-sant of Maharashtra (trad. 1270-1350), a bridging figure between Marathi Varkari devotion to Vithoba and the north Indian nirguna sant stream; his hymns entered the Sikh Adi Granth as bhagat bani alongside Kabir's, and later nirguna poets treated him as an honored forerunner.
figures.facet: The senior voice with two audiences: Namdev is claimed both by Maharashtra's saguna Vithoba-bhakti and by north India's nirguna sant devotion, a double reception that shows how porous the saguna/nirguna line the rest of this roster treats as firm actually was in practice.
figures.provenance: Namdev's abhangs (Varkari tradition) and Adi Granth bhagat bani; Christian Lee Novetzke, Religion and Public Memory: A Cultural History of Saint Namdev in India (2008).

figures.gloss: The Dharamdasi branch's central congregational ceremony: a square ritual space (chauka) is marked out and purified, a lamp is lit and offerings of coconut and betel are made, and mahants lead prayers over it — performed for initiation into the panth, for thanksgiving, and at death.
figures.facet: Ritual form for an anti-ritual founder: the historical Kabir mocked pilgrimage, idol, and rite alike, yet the organized panth carrying his name needed exactly this kind of repeatable congregational ceremony to survive as an institution across generations — form supplied where the founder refused to supply it.
figures.provenance: David N. Lorenzen's ethnographic accounts of Kabir Panth ritual practice (Kabir Legends and Ananta-Das's Kabir Parachai, 1991) and related fieldwork on Chhattisgarhi panthi communities.

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: the Shabd that Radhasoami systematized into a yoga is the sant poets' Word — Radhasoami's own genealogy names Kabir the fountainhead of Sant Mat, and the shared shabd/satguru vocabulary is continuous.
Intra-tradition bond: the panth's scripture is the poet's gathered voice — the Bijak is Kabir as book, with all the irony that canonization carries.
Intra-tradition bond: the weaver and the attributeless Ram he loved — nirguna bhakti's devotee-and-God pair, with no image permitted between them.
GOLD SEAM: several hundred of Kabir's hymns and couplets stand in the Adi Granth (compiled 1604) as bhagat bani — a documented textual inclusion; the seam is bound into the book itself.
GOLD SEAM: Nanak stands in the same nirguna sant stream as Kabir — one formless God, caste rejected, devotion over ritual (McLeod's scholarly consensus); no meeting between the two is attested.
GOLD SEAM: the same name, the avatar emptied — Kabir keeps 'Ram' while denying in his own verse that his Ram was ever born or wore a body; nirguna bhakti defined against the saguna avatar.

GOLD SEAM (held low): hagiography from Nabhadas's Bhaktamal (c. 1600) makes Kabir a disciple of the Vaishnava Ramananda, pulling him into the bhakti lineage — the chronology is strained and scholars doubt the link, so the tier stays at claim-strength.

GOLD SEAM (held low): Radhasoami's self-genealogy names Kabir the first of the Sants whose office its living masters continue — a retrospective lineage claim (Juergensmeyer 1991), not an initiatic chain.
Disciple and founder: Dharamdas's hereditary lineage at Damakheda is the branch through which most living Kabir Panth members trace their initiation.
The Anurag Sagar is cast as a dialogue between Kabir and Dharamdas, and remains the Dharamdasi branch's key scripture.
Sat Purush's cosmology of rescue from Kal is narrated in, and known chiefly through, the Anurag Sagar.

positive and negative poles of one cosmology — the Anurag Sagar's true God set against the counterfeit-creation lord Kal, whom Sant Mat's own roster already carries; a documented conduit from panthi myth into the Radhasoami stream.

Both name a true, ultimate Lord standing above and prior to the being who actively runs the visible creation — structurally parallel, though the panthi and Radhasoami systems formalize the hierarchy differently.
Ravidas's hymns stand in the Adi Granth (compiled 1604) as bhagat bani, the same documented textual inclusion that carries Kabir's own verses.
Namdev's hymns likewise stand in the Adi Granth as bhagat bani, predating Kabir's own inclusion by more than a century of oral and manuscript transmission.
Contemporaries in the low-caste nirguna devotional stream centered on Banaras, sharing vocabulary, audience, and social position though no meeting is attested.
Namdev is treated by the north Indian nirguna poets, Kabir included, as a senior forerunner voice, though he predates Kabir by roughly a century and worked in a different regional idiom.
The Dadu Panth institutionalizes devotion to the formless Ram in Kabir's own manner, and its anthologies are a primary channel through which Kabir's verse itself has been preserved and transmitted.
The chauka aarti is the central ritual of the Dharamdasi line Dharamdas founded.

dossier.essence: The devotional order organized around Kabir, the fifteenth-century weaver-poet of Banaras born into a low-caste Muslim julaha family, whose blunt vernacular verse preached devotion to one formless God while mocking the ritual apparatus of pandit and mullah alike. Kabirpanthis — drawn historically and still predominantly from low-caste and Dalit communities of North and Central India — follow him as satguru; the panth's two principal branches descend from the Kabir Chaura math in Varanasi and from Dharamdas in Chhattisgarh.
dossier.cosmos: Reality is one formless (nirguna) divine — Kabir names it Ram, Hari, Allah, Khuda interchangeably while denying it every attribute, including the avatar biography of the Vaishnava Rama. The soul transmigrates under karma, and liberation comes not by pilgrimage, rite, or caste standing but by inward devotion to the divine Word (shabda) awakened by the true guru. Later panth cosmology, elaborated in Dharamdasi texts such as the Anurag Sagar, hardens this into a drama of the true lord Satya Purush against Kal, the power of time and death who rules the lower creation.
dossier.practice: Practice centers on satsang and the singing of Kabir's songs and couplets (shabdas, sakhis, ramainis), initiation with a name from a guru or mahant, and commonly vegetarianism and abstinence from intoxicants. The panth is organized in monastic maths headed by mahants, with lay householder followers; in principle it rejects caste distinction, image worship, and ritualism, though observance across its branches is uneven — the recurring irony being an anti-institutional poet maintained by institutions.
dossier.canon: The Bijak, the eastern-recension scripture of the panth; the western Kabir Granthavali; several hundred of Kabir's hymns preserved as bhagat bani in the Sikh Adi Granth (compiled 1604); and, in the Dharamdasi branch, later works such as the Anurag Sagar.
dossier.reflects: Iconoclasm canonized — the drive to strip devotion down to the formless divine against image, rite, and caste, and the equal-and-opposite gravity by which even that refusal is organized into scripture, lineage, and sect.
sources: Linda Hess and Shukdev Singh, 'The Bijak of Kabir' (1983)
David N. Lorenzen, 'Kabir Legends and Ananta-das's Kabir Parachai' (1991)

links.cite: Linda Hess and Shukdev Singh, 'The Bijak of Kabir' (1983)
David N. Lorenzen, 'Kabir Legends and Ananta-das's Kabir Parachai' (1991)
